title: Balkan Barbershop — Live Client Site & Booking Platform
tagline: Portrait-driven editorial site fronting a full booking platform — bookings, payments, ratings, an admin AI assistant.
summary: Most barbershop websites fail on operations, not branding. The owner needed a system, not just a brochure. The result is a React 18 frontend with a brown/gold design system fronting a Node/Express booking platform with Stripe, JWT, Google OAuth, and a natural-language admin assistant gated by a metrics registry.
role_scope: Designer, engineer, deployer. Single-shop SaaS, four-month build.
resume_bullets: Shipped a React 18 + Node/Express booking platform with multi-service bookings, Stripe Checkout, JWT refresh-token rotation, Google OAuth, and a node-cron reminder pipeline.
Built an admin natural-language assistant that answers business questions in English, gated by a metrics registry, safety validator, and per-user rate limiter.
Migrated from AWS to a single DigitalOcean droplet with managed Postgres, cutting infra cost ~3x while keeping the same reliability bar.
demonstrates: Product-first design — killing a flashy feature when the math doesn't work, then bringing AI back where it earns its keep.
Owner-grade ops on a small SaaS — git-driven deploys, atomic releases, UFW + rate limiting, safe rollback path.
Honest engineering trade-offs (the SMS story) rather than perfection theater.
proof: 15+ PostgreSQL tables backing bookings, services, ratings, refresh tokens, waitlist entries, SMS DND, payment status.
outcomes: Production booking platform — customers book and pay, barbers see schedules and ratings, admins query the business in English.
constraints: Single-shop SaaS — designed for low ops burden, atomic deploy, single droplet.
Regulatory window on US SMS (A2P registration) — integration shipped switchable, safe-disabled by default.

case_study_sections.title: What I built
case_study_sections.summary: React 18 frontend with brown/gold design system, Framer Motion transitions on core pages, Recharts admin analytics, react-toastify notifications. Node/Express backend with JWT + refresh-token rotation, Google OAuth, PostgreSQL across 15+ tables (users, services, bookings, booking_services, barber_services, refresh_tokens, rating_results, sms_dnd_numbers, waitlist_entries). Booking lifecycle features: multi-service bookings, per-booking ratings, reschedule and cancel with timezone awareness, node-cron reminder pipeline. Stripe Checkout with webhook-driven payment status and a 'Pay Now' flow for post-booking collection. Admin LLM assistant — natural-language queries against the business data ('how many bookings this month?', 'revenue by barber?') gated by a metrics registry, safety validator, and per-user rate limiter. A safe-deploy script with git-readiness checks, commit-marker backend-change detection, SSH multiplexing, and UFW rate limiting on the droplet.

case_study_sections.title: One sharp decision
case_study_sections.summary: Killed the AI hairstyle generator and virtual try-on in week two. The initial commit shipped DALL·E hairstyle gen, a TensorFlow.js / Replicate try-on, a Python/Flask AI backend, and 4,800+ lines of supporting code. By day four the math was clear: infrastructure burden was high, value to a working barber was low, and the booking system itself wasn't done. Cut it cleanly, focused on the booking core for two months, and only re-introduced AI months later as a backend admin assistant — where it actually shortens the operator's day. Same project, second AI attempt, ten times the ROI.

case_study_sections.title: One real struggle
case_study_sections.summary: SMS. I oscillated AWS SNS → Twilio → AWS SNS over five days. SNS can't do custom Sender IDs for US numbers; Twilio can but costs more and A2P registration is its own slog. The honest framing: I didn't solve SMS. I stopped pretending I could ship it under the current regulatory window, made the integration switchable, and kept email and in-app notifications doing the work. That same pass drove the bigger AWS → DigitalOcean migration.

case_study_sections.title: Outcome
case_study_sections.summary: Production booking platform on a DigitalOcean droplet with managed Postgres, Nginx with SSL, UFW firewall, git-push-to-deploy. Customers book, pay, get reminders; barbers see their schedule and ratings; admins query the business in English.
